target: data/sections.json part1.html — "evidence-roadmap" div, Tier 3 paragraph reframed to lead with strength
hole_addressed: moderate: Tier 3 description apologized for our own evidence by previewing the dome defender’s "measured from the wrong frame" objection. Reframe to lead with strength while preserving the hierarchy logic.
find_text: Tier 3 — Kill-shot binary tests and falsification tests (Parts 5 and 7): Structured tests where the dome and the globe make different, measurable predictions. These are powerful but rely on external datasets (satellite measurements, GPS timing, stellar parallax) that a determined defender could dispute as "measured from the wrong frame." The self-contradiction arguments in Tier 1 face no such objection.
replace_text: Tier 3 — Kill-shot binary tests and falsification tests (Parts 5 and 7): Structured tests where the dome and the globe make different, measurable predictions, and independent datasets decide between them. Gaia parallax of 1.8 billion stars, the ~38 µs/day atomic-clock correction that makes GPS work, and Southern Hemisphere great-circle distances each yield quantities the dome geometry cannot reproduce without rebuilding its own parameters. These arguments rely on measurements the dome did not choose; the Tier 1 self-contradictions in Parts 2 and 2b go one step further by needing no external data at all, using only the dome’s own equations.
replacement_html:
How This Review Is Organized

This review applies a three-tier structure organized by the type of argument rather than by topic, because argument type determines how hard a conclusion is to escape. The dome model’s own stated priority is prospective prediction (‘The strongest claim this registry makes is prospective prediction’ — wins page). Our tier system is organized by a different axis: how hard each argument type is to escape, from internally self-contradictory through externally falsified:

Tier 1 — Self-contradictions (Parts 2 and 2b): Arguments derived entirely from the dome’s own stated parameters and equations. When a model’s geometry predicts a Schumann frequency of ~22 Hz but the observed value is 7.83 Hz, or when a local moon at 2,534 km produces one tidal spike instead of the observed two-bulge pattern, no external data is needed and no frame-of-reference objection applies. The dome’s own mathematics refutes the dome. These are the hardest arguments to escape.

Tier 2 — Point-by-point evaluation (Part 3): Each of the dome’s claimed wins assessed individually against published data and internal consistency. Many referenced observations are real — the verdicts address whether the dome’s interpretation holds up, not whether the underlying data exists.

Tier 3 — Kill-shot binary tests and falsification tests (Parts 5 and 7): Structured tests where the dome and the globe make different, measurable predictions, and independent datasets decide between them. Gaia parallax of 1.8 billion stars, the ~38 µs/day atomic-clock correction that makes GPS work, and Southern Hemisphere great-circle distances each yield quantities the dome geometry cannot reproduce without rebuilding its own parameters. These arguments rely on measurements the dome did not choose; the Tier 1 self-contradictions in Parts 2 and 2b go one step further by needing no external data at all, using only the dome’s own equations.

This ordering is deliberate. A reader who reads only Parts 2 and 2b will find the model already refutes itself — Part 2 from the dome’s own geometry, Part 2b from the dome’s own automation code. The remaining parts confirm and extend that conclusion with independent evidence from progressively broader domains.
holes_addressed: Tier 3 no longer leads with vulnerability. The new text names three concrete external-data kill-shots (Gaia parallax, GPS clock correction, Southern-Hemisphere great-circle distances) and frames them as independently decisive.

The "determined defender could dispute as measured from the wrong frame" clause is removed; the preserved final sentence still conveys Tier 1’s categorical strength ("needing no external data at all") without signal-boosting the dome defender’s escape hatch.
Tier 1 primacy retained: the paragraph now ends by pointing forward/back to Tier 1 as going one step further, so the ordering logic is intact.
new_evidence_added: Gaia DR3 parallax catalogue of ~1.8 billion stars (referenced in Parts 5/7)
GPS atomic-clock GR+SR correction of ~38 µs/day (referenced in Parts 5/7)
Southern Hemisphere great-circle distances (e.g. Sydney–Santiago) vs dome-disc distances (Part 5 Test 7 and Part 4)

anticipated_objections.objection: Dome defender: "Gaia/GPS/Southern-Hemisphere distances are themselves model-dependent; they assume a spherical Earth, so they cannot refute a spherical Earth without begging the question."
anticipated_objections.response: Gaia parallax is a geometric measurement, not a spherical-Earth derivation — it measures the angular shift of a star as Earth moves across a baseline. Any flat-disc reinterpretation must reproduce the same angular shifts; none has. GPS timing corrections are empirically necessary for the system to work at all — the ~38 µs/day figure is observationally measured, not theoretically imposed. Great-circle distances are measured by non-stop flights and submarine cables, both of which are operational facts. Tier 3 arguments are robust to the "wrong frame" escape, which is precisely why the curmudgeon recommended removing the apologetic framing.

anticipated_objections.objection: Dome defender: "Calling Tier 3 ‘independently decisive’ while also saying Tier 1 ‘goes further’ is contradictory. Either Tier 3 is decisive on its own or it isn’t."
anticipated_objections.response: Both statements are simultaneously true. Tier 3 decides the question given ordinary trust in external datasets. Tier 1 decides the question without requiring any trust in external datasets — a strictly stronger epistemic position. The roadmap is explicit that the axis is "how hard each argument type is to escape," and Tier 1 is harder to escape by construction.

anticipated_objections.objection: Dome defender: "Naming only three kill-shots privileges them over the many other Tier 3 arguments in Parts 5 and 7."
anticipated_objections.response: Three examples are illustrative, matching the format already used for Tier 1 (Schumann, two-bulge tides) and Tier 2 (no examples — reader is directed to Part 3). Full enumeration lives in Parts 5 and 7. The examples chosen are the most pedagogically accessible and most difficult to dismiss as "frame-dependent."

kernel_of_truth: The original Tier 3 paragraph was epistemologically honest — external data IS in principle disputable by a defender who rejects the globe frame — and that honesty is why EXP-091 phrased it that way. Our review should not sand down that nuance by pretending kill-shots are frame-free. The fix preserves the substance (Tier 1 is categorically stronger because it needs no external data) while removing the rhetorical posture that invites the dome defender into the text. Strength is communicated by naming concrete evidence, not by previewing objections to it.
retained_text_note: The critical Steelman Evidence Ladder misattribution flagged by the curmudgeon review is already fixed in the current sections.json — the opening sentence now reads "This review applies a three-tier structure organized by the type of argument" with no "Steelman Evidence Ladder" phrase. Only the Tier 3 framing (ISS-920) remains to address.
progressive_disclosure_note: The roadmap div is intentionally NOT wrapped in <details>/<summary> — it is an orientation block that must be permanently visible (EXP-091 design intent). No TLDR required.
decider_guidance:
Minimal-diff option: apply find_and_replace on the Tier 3

. Full-diff option: replace the entire evidence-roadmap

with replacement_html. Both produce the same end state; find_and_replace is lower-risk.
